items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, selecting an extension row immediately closes the picker and triggers enable or disable.

If that enable/disable request fails, the user gets no visible error in the Quick Access flow.

Visible result:

- open **Quick Access -> Extensions**,
- select an extension row,
- make the enable/disable request fail,
- the picker closes anyway,
- no inline error, banner, or retry state is shown in the Quick Access surface.

So the action can fail, but the mounted picker disappears without explaining what happened.

### Root Cause

The mounted `ext ` provider treats selecting an extension row as an immediate enable/disable action:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1108-1117`
  - `show` items call `extensions.disableExtension(...)` or `extensions.enableExtension(...)`

The generic Quick Access accept path always closes the picker right after invoking the provider action:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1258-1263`
  - calls `active.provider.onAccept(item)`
  - then unconditionally calls `hide()`

The actual enable/disable operations catch errors, but only store them in `ExtensionsContext` state and log to console:

- `/src/context/ExtensionsContext.tsx:517-526`
  - failed enable sets `error`
  - logs `Failed to enable extension:`

- `/src/context/ExtensionsContext.tsx:530-539`
  - failed disable sets `error`
  - logs `Failed to disable extension:`

But the mounted **Quick Access -> Extensions** picker does not render `extensions.error()` anywhere before it hides.

So a failed toggle request closes the picker, while the only recorded failure state lives outside the Quick Access UI.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0964ad96-9282-4518-8b50-03f3a75e4c99

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Quick Access**.
2. Enter **Extensions** mode.
3. Select an extension row so the picker attempts to enable or disable it.
4. Cause that backend request to fail.
5. Observe the Quick Access UI after the failed action.


### Expected Behavior

If enable/disable fails from **Quick Access -> Extensions**, the user should get visible feedback in that same flow.

Examples:

- keep the picker open and show an error,
- show a visible error banner before closing,
- or otherwise surface the failure clearly in-context.


### Actual Behavior

The picker closes immediately after selection even when the enable/disable request fails.

The mounted Quick Access flow shows no visible error or retry state.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Quick Access Extensions issues:

- `#33291` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text**
- `#33403` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions**

Those reports are about search behavior.

This report is about the separate failure-handling path for row selection in **Quick Access -> Extensions**.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Quick Access Extensions failure`
- `Quick Access Extensions no visible error`
- `Quick Access Extensions enable disable fails`
- `Quick Access Extensions closes immediately on failed`
- local grep for the `ext ` provider `onAccept`, the generic `handleAccept(...); hide()` path, and `enableExtension` / `disableExtension` failure handling

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Quick Access Extensions failed enable or disable closes the picker with no visible error** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact `Quick Access -> Extensions` failure-visibility bug at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issues are different:
  - `#33291` `Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text`
  - `#33403` `Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions`
- Why they are different:
  - those issues cover search/filter mismatches,
  - this report is about failed enable/disable actions closing the picker with no visible error in the mounted Quick Access flow.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] BreadcrumbsPicker search can match hidden truncated names not visible in result rows
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In the breadcrumb picker, long file or symbol names are visually truncated in the result rows, but the search box still matches the hidden tail of the full underlying name.

Visible result:

- open a breadcrumb folder picker or symbol picker with a long item name,
- the row label is truncated with ellipsis,
- search for a word from the hidden tail of that long name,
- the row still appears,
- but the visible result row does not show the searched word anywhere.

So the picker can return a match with no visible matching text in the row.

### Root Cause

The picker search indexes the full `item.name` string for both modes:

- folder mode:
  - `src/components/editor/breadcrumbs/BreadcrumbsPicker.tsx:21-24`
- symbol mode:
  - `src/components/editor/breadcrumbs/BreadcrumbsPicker.tsx:25-28`

But the rendered row labels are visually truncated:

- folder label:
  - `src/components/editor/breadcrumbs/BreadcrumbsPicker.tsx:209-217`
  - `overflow: "hidden"`
  - `"text-overflow": "ellipsis"`
  - `"white-space": "nowrap"`
- symbol label:
  - `src/components/editor/breadcrumbs/BreadcrumbsPicker.tsx:264-273`
  - `overflow: "hidden"`
  - `"text-overflow": "ellipsis"`
  - `"white-space": "nowrap"`

There is no tooltip or secondary full-text surface in the picker row to reveal the hidden tail.

So search operates on full hidden text, while the result row only exposes a shortened label.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1274d989-cfcc-42e0-a5bb-7afdcc3f8bd6

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a breadcrumb folder picker or symbol picker containing at least one long item name.
2. Make sure that row is truncated visually with ellipsis.
3. Type a substring that appears only in the hidden tail of that long name.
4. Observe the filtered results.


### Expected Behavior

If a result matches the search query, the visible row should expose the matching text somehow.

At minimum, search should not return rows where the matching text is entirely hidden from the user.


### Actual Behavior

The picker filters against the full underlying name, not the truncated visible label.

That allows a row to match a hidden substring that the visible result row does not show.


### Additional Context

- Distinct from remote issue `#27216` (**Breadcrumbs segment label truncated at 120px with no title tooltip showing full name**). That report is about truncation in the breadcrumb bar itself. This issue is about the separate breadcrumb picker search returning matches from hidden truncated row text.
- Distinct from remote issue `#30186` (**Breadcrumbs path segments and symbol names truncated with no hover tooltip**). That report is about truncated breadcrumb display outside the picker. This issue is about picker search semantics.
- Distinct from local report [bug-report-breadcrumb-symbol-picker-search-ignores-visible-symbol-kind-text.md](/opt/_177_/bugs-codex/bug-report-breadcrumb-symbol-picker-search-ignores-visible-symbol-kind-text.md). That report is about visible kind text being ignored by search. This issue is the opposite mismatch: search can match hidden text that is not visibly shown.
- Distinct from local report [bug-report-breadcrumbs-picker-hovering-a-row-changes-the-enter-target-without-clicking.md](/opt/_177_/bugs-codex/bug-report-breadcrumbs-picker-hovering-a-row-changes-the-enter-target-without-clicking.md). That report is about hover retargeting `Enter`. This issue is about search/result visibility mismatch.
- Root-cause class: **`BreadcrumbsPicker` filters against full `item.name`, but the visible row label is truncated with ellipsis and has no way to reveal the hidden matching tail**

### Duplicate Check

Checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and remote (`platformnetwork/bounty-challenge`, open + closed) with:

- `breadcrumb picker hidden text search`
- `breadcrumbs picker truncated name search`
- `breadcrumb picker hidden tail`
- `breadcrumb picker search can match hidden`
- local grep for `breadcrumb picker`, `truncated`, `hidden`, `search can match`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **BreadcrumbsPicker search can match hidden truncated names not visible in result rows** path.
- Remote: no matching issue found in `platformnetwork/bounty-challenge` for this specific breadcrumb-picker hidden-tail search mismatch at check time (2026-03-19 UTC).
- Nearby live issues are different:
  - `#27216` truncated breadcrumb bar labels with no tooltip
  - `#30186` truncated breadcrumb segments/symbol names outside the picker
  - `#33326` visible symbol kind text ignored by picker search

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] ConfigureDefaultEditorDialog modal uses hardcoded width 480px instead of design token

labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

items.body: ### Project ide ### Description The ConfigureDefaultEditorDialog modal container uses a hardcoded `width: "480px"` (line 91) instead of a design system token. Other properties in the same style object correctly use design tokens: `border-radius: "var(--jb-radius-lg)"` (line 86) and `box-shadow: "var(--jb-shadow-modal)"` (line 87). The hardcoded pixel width is inconsistent with the component's own token usage pattern. ### Error Message ```shell N/A — design token inconsistency ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # src/components/ConfigureDefaultEditorDialog.tsx lines 85-93: const modalStyle: JSX.CSSProperties = { background: "var(--jb-modal)", # uses token "border-radius": "var(--jb-radius-lg)", # uses token "box-shadow": "var(--jb-shadow-modal)", # uses token display: "flex", "flex-direction": "column", "max-height": "80vh", width: "480px", # HARDCODED — should use token "max-width": "90vw", overflow: "hidden", }; ``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/ConfigureDefaultEditorDialog.tsx, line 91 ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE. 2. Right-click a file in the explorer and select "Open With > Configure Default Editor..." 3. Observe the dialog opens at a fixed 480px width. 4. Inspect the DOM element — width is set as an inline `480px` value, not a CSS variable. ### Expected Behavior The modal width should use a design system token (e.g., `var(--jb-modal-width-sm)` or `var(--modal-width)`) consistent with how other properties in the same style object use tokens. ### Actual Behavior The modal width is hardcoded to `480px` at line 91, while `border-radius` (line 86) and `box-shadow` (line 87) in the same style object use design tokens `var(--jb-radius-lg)` and `var(--jb-shadow-modal)`. This creates a design system inconsistency where the modal cannot be resized through token changes.

items.title: 📺 Bounty: Roku/Fire TV App for BoTTube (125 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Build a BoTTube TV client targeting Roku and Fire TV so users can browse and watch BoTTube videos from a television-focused interface.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is about opening a new viewing surface for BoTTube, not just shipping a mock TV shell. A real TV client expands reach, tests lean-back consumption, and makes the platform feel more like a full video destination.

## Scope
- build a TV-oriented BoTTube client for Roku, Fire TV, or both
- support browsing at least a meaningful subset of BoTTube content such as trending, recent, or category-based feeds
- support full-screen playback of BoTTube videos
- document the platform target, build path, and any account or developer-mode assumptions
- provide a working review path for maintainers, such as side-load instructions, package, or demo video
- include voice search only if the platform target realistically supports it within the implementation

## Deliverable
A working TV app or prototype-quality client that can browse and play BoTTube videos on at least one television platform, with enough documentation for maintainers to test or review it.

## Platform Targets
Acceptable targets include:
- Roku
- Fire TV
- both, if the implementation is shared cleanly or the reward justifies the added surface

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] app runs on at least one supported TV platform
- [ ] users can browse BoTTube videos through a TV-appropriate navigation flow
- [ ] videos can be played back full screen
- [ ] setup or sideload instructions are documented clearly
- [ ] source code is shared publicly through a repo, PR, or equivalent reference
- [ ] maintainers can review the app through a package, build instructions, or demo evidence
- [ ] platform assumptions and known limitations are documented clearly

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- target platform implemented
- repo, PR, or source link
- build or sideload instructions
- screenshots or short demo video of browsing and playback
- notes on any platform limitations or unsupported features

## Payout
`125 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- a mock UI with no real video playback
- vague “TV support” planning without a runnable client
- unrelated mobile or web UI work
- forcing both Roku and Fire TV if only one working target can be delivered well
- undocumented platform-specific hacks that maintainers cannot review

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for TV app implementation proof and payout discussion. If code lives in another repo, link it back here and keep demo, build, and verification details on this issue.

## Notes
This issue was normalized to the conservative payout already shown in the title. The earlier body text referenced a higher reward, but the working payout for review should be treated as `125 RTC` unless explicitly changed later by maintainers.

items.title: 🤖 Bounty: Create a Unique Bot Personality That Gets 50+ Subscribers (50 RTC)
items.body:
## Bounty: Create a Unique Bot Personality That Gets 50+ Subscribers

**Reward:** 50 RTC  
**Difficulty:** Beginner  
**Category:** Community / Creative  

### Description

This is the easiest bounty on BoTTube — build a bot with a creative personality, upload some videos, and get 50 subscribers. No coding experience required if you use the API directly via curl or the upcoming SDK.

### Requirements

1. **Register a bot** on https://bottube.ai with a unique name and personality
2. **Upload at least 5 videos** (can be short clips, screen recordings, AI-generated — anything creative)
3. **Write engaging descriptions** that match your bot's personality
4. **Get 50+ subscribers** (other bots and humans can subscribe)
5. **Interact** — reply to comments on your videos, comment on others' videos

### What Makes a Great Bot Personality?

The best bots on BoTTube have a distinct voice. Some ideas:
- A retro gaming reviewer who only talks in 80s slang
- A cooking bot that gives recipes but always gets one ingredient hilariously wrong
- A motivational speaker bot that's overly intense about mundane topics
- A nature documentary narrator for everyday objects
- A time traveler bot confused by modern technology


### Quality Bar (Effective Feb 10, 2026)

We are paying to make BoTTube better. **Low-effort filler does not count** toward bounty requirements and will be **disqualified**, including:
- solid-color / gradient “aesthetic” clips
- basic geometric loops / CSS test patterns / bouncing shapes
- near-duplicate uploads where only a color/word changes
- spam/burst uploads intended to farm bounties

What counts:
- videos with a clear concept (teach something, tell a joke, show a process, real AI art with prompts, real data viz with sources)
- meaningful variation across frames and across uploads
- titles + descriptions that explain what the viewer is seeing

If your submission is rejected for quality, you can **try again**: upload improved videos and reply with the updated links.

### Verification
- We'll check subscriber count via the API
- Subscribers must be from different accounts (no self-subscribing from alt accounts)
- Bot must have been active for at least 7 days

### Bonus Tiers
| Milestone | Reward |
|-----------|--------|
| 50 subscribers | 50 RTC |
| 100 subscribers | 100 RTC |
| 250 subscribers | 200 RTC |
| Most subscribed new bot of the month | 300 RTC |

### How to Claim
1. Comment on this issue with your bot's name and a link to its profile
2. Once you hit 50 subscribers, comment again with proof
3. Include your RTC wallet address

items.title: [FEATURE] Remove Unused SM2 Algorithm
items.body:
## Background

The java-tron codebase contains a complete SM2 (Chinese Commercial Cryptographic Standard) implementation in `crypto` module. The `SignUtils.java` provides a configuration flag `isECKeyCryptoEngine` to switch between ECKey (SECP256K1) and SM2.

## Problem Statement

1. **SM2 is never used in production**: All production deployments use `ECKey (SECP256K1)`. The `isECKeyCryptoEngine` flag is always `true`.

2. **Dead code maintenance burden**: Maintaining ~1,100+ lines of unused cryptographic code increases complexity, testing surface, and audit scope.

3. **Incompatible with TRON network**: TRON uses `SECP256K1` for all transactions. `SM2` would not work with existing accounts.

4. **Bug Bounty false positives**: The unused SM2 code frequently triggers false positive reports in bug bounty programs. Security researchers often flag SM2-related code as potential vulnerabilities, requiring time-consuming investigation and response despite the code never being used in production.

## Rationale

### Why should this feature exist?

Removing unused code improves code quality and reduces maintenance overhead.

### What are the use cases?

1. Developers will have cleaner codebase to work with
2. Security audits will focus on actually-used cryptography
3. Fewer false positive reports in bug bounty programs


## Scope of Impact

- [ ] Core protocol
- [ ] API/RPC
- [ ] Database
- [ ] Network layer
- [ ] Smart contracts
- [x] Documentation
- [x] Other: crypto module, keystore

**Breaking Changes:**
None for production users. Only affects code using `isECKeyCryptoEngine=false` (which doesn't exist in production).

**Backward Compatibility:**
Fully backward compatible - no production behavior changes.

## Implementation

**Are you willing to implement this feature?**
- [x] Yes, I can implement this feature

labels.name: Needs Reproducing

items.body:
### Client Version:

516.1667

### Issue Summary:

During this round, a bomb went off in medical. Understandably, this temporarily deafened me. When the deafness effect from ear damage wore, off, and I could see the text from people talking again, as well as scanning to see my ears perfectly fine, my game itself remained muted. No sounds would play. Even carrying into the next round, having reconnected without closing & reopening my window, my game remained muted. items.title: [BUG] [alpha] Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions
items.body:
### Project

ide

### Description

In **Quick Access -> Extensions**, the action rows visibly show labels like **Install Extension...** and **Refresh Extensions**.

But searching for those same visible phrases can return no results.

Examples:

- `install extension`
- `refresh extensions`

So the picker can visibly name an action, but the search logic does not reliably match the full visible label the user is typing.

### Root Cause

The mounted **Extensions** provider in Quick Access uses hardcoded keyword checks instead of filtering against the action rows' actual visible label/description text:

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1075-1078`
  - action section is only shown when the query matches short hardcoded fragments:
  - `"install".includes(trimmedQuery)`
  - `"marketplace".includes(trimmedQuery)`
  - `"refresh".includes(trimmedQuery)`

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1081-1088`
  - the visible row label is **Install Extension...**

- `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx:1091-1097`
  - the visible row label is **Refresh Extensions**

That means longer real queries based on the visible text fail.

For example:

- `install extension`
  - does not satisfy `"install".includes("install extension")`
- `refresh extensions`
  - does not satisfy `"refresh".includes("refresh extensions")`

So the action visibility logic is narrower than the text the UI actually renders.


### Error Message

```shell

```

### Debug Logs

```shell

```

### System Information

```shell
- Cortex IDE `alpha`
- Platform: any
```

### Screenshots

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f68375c8-98ce-47d7-ade6-ecba7cd8531e

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open **Quick Access**.
2. Enter **Extensions** mode.
3. In the search field, type `install extension`.
4. Observe whether **Install Extension...** appears.
5. Clear the query.
6. Type `refresh extensions`.
7. Observe whether **Refresh Extensions** appears.


### Expected Behavior

If an action row visibly says **Install Extension...** or **Refresh Extensions**, searching those visible phrases should return the matching action.


### Actual Behavior

The search only checks a few short hardcoded keywords, so longer natural queries based on the visible action labels can return no results.


### Additional Context

This is not the same as the current live Quick Access Extensions issue:

- `#33291` **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text**

That report is about visible **Enabled / Disabled** row status text.

This report is about the separate **Install Extension...** and **Refresh Extensions** action rows not being discoverable by their own visible labels.

### Duplicate Check

Re-checked local (`bug-3`, `bugs-codex`) and targeted live repo searches with:

- `Quick Access Extensions Install Extension`
- `Quick Access Extensions Refresh Extensions`
- `Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action`
- `Quick Access Extensions install extension search`
- `Quick Access Extensions refresh extensions search`
- local grep for `prefix: "ext "`, `Install Extension...`, `Refresh Extensions`, and the hardcoded `includes(trimmedQuery)` checks in `/src/context/QuickAccessContext.tsx`

Findings:

- Local: no matching report found for this specific **Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible action labels like Install Extension and Refresh Extensions** path.
- Live repo: no matching issue found in `PlatformNetwork/bounty-challenge` for this exact `Quick Access -> Extensions` action-label search mismatch at check time (`2026-03-19 UTC`).
- Closest live issue is different:
  - `#33291` `Quick Access Extensions search ignores visible Enabled or Disabled status text`
- Why it is different:
  - `#33291` is about visible enabled-state text on extension rows,
  - this report is about the separate visible action labels for **Install Extension...** and **Refresh Extensions** not participating in search.

items.title: [BOUNTY] Upload Your First 10 Videos — Earn 15 RTC (Pool: 750 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Upload your first `10` real videos to BoTTube and claim milestone rewards at `5` and `10` uploads.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is for creator onboarding and content growth. It only works if the uploads are real, varied, and useful to the platform rather than filler posted to hit a count target.

## Milestones
- upload `5` accepted videos: `5 RTC`
- upload `10` accepted videos total: `15 RTC` total

## Scope
- upload videos through the site or API
- ensure each video has a title and description
- cover at least `3` different topics or themes across the first `10` videos
- meet the current quality bar
- keep your profile public enough for maintainers to verify the uploads

## Quality Bar
Rejected content includes:
- blank, black, or near-empty clips
- solid-color or gradient filler
- basic geometric loops or test-pattern spam
- near-duplicate uploads with only tiny wording or color changes
- burst uploads intended only to farm the bounty

Accepted content should show a real concept, joke, process, demo, tutorial, visual idea, or creative theme.

## Deliverable
A BoTTube creator profile with `5` or `10` accepted videos that meet the quality bar and show meaningful content variation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] uploaded videos are live and reviewable on BoTTube
- [ ] each video has a title and description
- [ ] at least `3` different topics or themes are represented across the first `10` videos
- [ ] uploads pass the quality bar and are not near-duplicates
- [ ] claim includes the creator profile URL
- [ ] maintainers can verify the milestone directly from the profile

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- your BoTTube profile URL
- whether you are claiming the `5`-video or `10`-video milestone
- links or IDs for the videos if needed for quick verification
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `5 RTC` at `5` accepted videos
- `15 RTC` total at `10` accepted videos
- pool: `750 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- filler or low-effort uploads that fail the quality bar
- duplicate batches from the same claimant under multiple profiles
- unverifiable profiles or deleted uploads
- claiming milestones without the required video count live on-platform

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for creator milestone proof and payout review. If the upload flow itself fails or profile counts are incorrect, open a separate product issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

items.title: 🏭 Bounty T1: GitHub Webhook Receiver
items.body:
## 🟢 Tier 1 Bounty — Open Race

**Reward:** 250 $FNDRY
**Deadline:** 72h | **Min review score:** 6/10

### Description
Build the GitHub webhook handler that receives PR and issue events, updates bounty status in the database, and triggers the review pipeline.

### Requirements
- [ ] `POST /webhooks/github` endpoint with signature verification (HMAC-SHA256)
- [ ] Handle events: `pull_request` (opened, closed, merged, synchronize), `issues` (opened, closed, labeled)
- [ ] On PR opened: match to bounty issue via `Closes #N`, update bounty status to `in_review`
- [ ] On PR merged: update bounty status to `completed`, trigger payout webhook
- [ ] On issue labeled `bounty`: auto-create bounty record in DB
- [ ] Event logging table: event_type, payload_hash, processed_at, status
- [ ] Idempotency — don't process the same delivery_id twice
- [ ] Tests with mock webhook payloads

### Tech Stack
FastAPI + PostgreSQL + hmac verification

items.body:
## Bounty: GitHub Actions CI/CD Pipeline

**Tier:** 1 (Open Race) | **Reward:** 100 $FNDRY | **Timeout:** 72h

---

### Description

Set up the CI/CD pipeline for the SolFoundry monorepo — linting, testing, building, and deploying.

### Requirements

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — runs on every PR:
  - Lint (ESLint for TS, Ruff for Python, Clippy for Rust)
  - Type check (tsc --noEmit)
  - Unit tests (Jest/Vitest for frontend, pytest for backend)
  - Build check (next build, cargo build)
- `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` — runs on merge to main:
  - Build frontend → deploy to Vercel or DO droplet
  - Build backend → deploy Docker container to DO droplet
- `.github/workflows/anchor.yml` — Solana contract checks:
  - `anchor build` + `anchor test` on devnet
- Branch protection rules documented
- PR template (`.github/pull_request_template.md`)

### Deliverables

- Workflow YAML files in `.github/workflows/`
- PR template
- Documentation on how the pipeline works

### How to Submit

Fork → implement → open PR against `main`

**Open race — first valid PR wins 100 $FNDRY**

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
- timestamp
- image analysis
- sensor data
- community verification
- agent validation

This prevents cheating.

---

# Local Community Hunts

Cities can host events.

Example:

City tourism hunt.

Participants must:

- visit landmarks
- photograph locations
- answer trivia questions
- collect digital artifacts

Players earn rewards and achievements.

---

# Mystery Hunts

Communities can build complex puzzles.

Example:

ARG style mystery.

Players decode clues across:

- posts
- locations
- cryptography puzzles
- hidden objects

Winning teams receive large rewards.

---

# Creator Economy

Creators earn money by creating:

- hunts
- puzzles
- games
- community events
- meme challenges

Popular hunts generate revenue.

---

# Sponsor Hunts

Businesses can sponsor hunts.

Example:

Coffee shop hunt.

Players must:

- visit store
- photograph special item
- answer trivia

Players receive rewards.

Businesses gain foot traffic.

---

# Reputation

Players build reputation through:

- discoveries
- successful hunts
- puzzle solving
- content creation
- community votes

High reputation unlocks larger hunts.

---

# Global Leaderboards

Leaderboards include:

- top explorers
- top creators
- top puzzle solvers
- top meme creators
- top communities

---

# Digital Artifacts

Players can collect artifacts.

Examples:

- rare meme templates
- historical discoveries
- puzzle trophies
- city badges

Artifacts can be traded.

---

# AR Integration

Augmented reality can display:

- hidden objects
- digital creatures
- puzzle clues
- collectible artifacts

Players explore cities like a game.

---

# Real World Missions

Large missions could include:

- documenting environmental changes
- mapping hiking trails
- identifying plants
- reporting infrastructure issues

Users earn money for useful discoveries.

---

# Community Worlds

Communities can create themed worlds.

Examples:

- paranormal hunters
- abandoned place explorers
- architecture photography
- meme creators
- puzzle solvers

Each world has its own hunts and rewards.

---

# Money Flow

Money enters the system from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community pools
- creators
- brands
- city governments

Players earn rewards by participating.

---

# Why People Would Instantly Use It

It solves multiple things people already love:

People love:

- social media
- viral challenges
- scavenger hunts
- puzzles
- memes
- competition
- earning money online

This combines them into one platform.

---

# Why FRAME Makes It Better

FRAME enables:

- verifiable rewards
- identity ownership
- fair payouts
- portable reputation
- no platform lock-in
- decentralized moderation

Users keep their audience and earnings.

---

# Long Term Evolution

Over time the system becomes:

- global discovery network
- collaborative knowledge system
- real world exploration game
- meme economy
- bounty network

People earn money by **being curious, creative, and observant**.

---

# End Vision

The app turns the real world into a **giant interactive game**.

People explore cities.

Solve puzzles.

Create memes.

Document strange discoveries.

Earn money.

And build communities around curiosity and creativity.
